insurance provider, the responsible third-party should pay first. NYS Medicaid pays the lesser of Patient
Responsibility (PR) or the NYS Medicaid fee, regardless of the PR amount.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update May 2023 Volume 39 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsels the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation.
• Delivery confirmation must be maintained by the pharmacy for six years from the date of payment and
must be retrievable upon audit.
• All shipping and delivery costs are the responsibility of the pharmacy.
• Medicaid members cannot be charged for delivery if Medicaid reimburses for all, or any portion of the item
being delivered.
• The pharmacy is accountable for proper delivery of intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled,
defective, or otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• The pharmacy is liable for the cost of any item damaged or lost through distribution and delivery.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement
Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the
Medicare Program. Medicaid pharmacy enrollment information can be accessed online at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/ProviderEnrollment/index.aspx.
Analogous to TPL in the section above, Medicare benefits must also be maximized prior to billing Medicaid. Services
covered by both Medicare and Medicaid must first be billed to Medicare (See below for Parts A, B, D).
It is the pharmacy’s responsibility:
• To bill the appropriate Medicare benefit.
• To notify both the member and the prescriber of any needed authorizations or changes the Medicare Plan
requires.
Pharmacy providers can bill Medicaid only after payment information is received from Medicare. Pharmacy providers
may not circumvent Medicare billing procedures for a Medicare covered member and must bill by the Healthcare
Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code (NDC) as the Medicare Plan requires.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_30.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents emphasize the importance of coordination of benefits and timely billing.
* Provider manual rules require pharmacies to maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid requires providers to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid. Pharmacies must ensure accurate COB submissions and accept other patient responsibility amounts.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Reminders: • It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. • It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.]

Medicaid is always the payer of last resort, and federal regulations require that all other available resources be used before NYS Medicaid considers payment.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4] - [Chunk ID: mu_no04_apr25_pr.pdf::0025]-[pages: [7, 8]] - [Chunk Content: April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7 Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing Instruction for New York State Medicaid New York State (NYS) Medicaid providers are required to bill applicable third parties that may be liable for a claim before billing NYS Medicaid.]

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacies enrolled in the Medicaid Program as a billing provider are required to demonstrate participation in the Medicare Program. Pharmacies must maximize Medicare benefits prior to billing Medicaid.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0065]-[pages: [17]] - [Chunk Content: Medicare/Medicaid Reimbursement]

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both Medicaid policy documents and provider manual rules emphasize the importance of coordination of benefits.
* However, Medicaid policy documents provide more specific guidance on the process of coordination of benefits.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Reminders: • It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. • It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.]

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2025 Volume 41 Number 4] - [Chunk ID: mu_no04_apr25_pr.pdf::0025]-[pages: [7, 8]] - [Chunk Content: April 2025 New York State Medicaid Update pg. 7 Pharmacy Reminder: Coordination of Benefits Processing Instruction for New York State Medicaid]

test_id_31.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for coinsurance, deductibles, and balance billing for dual eligible members.
test_id_31.concept: coinsurance deductibles and balance billing for dual eligible members

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only Coverage Reminder
When providing services to Medicaid recipients enrolled with Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only
coverage, Medicaid will only consider reimbursement of patient responsibility amounts of Medicare
coinsurance and/or deductible amounts.
Additionally, if Medicare did not approve the claims, or services are provided that are not statutorily covered
by Medicare, then the secondary claims submitted to Medicaid will be denied for:
• Edit 01027 (Medicaid Coverage code “09”-Medicare approved Amount Missing)
• Claim Adjustment Reason Code “16”,
• Remark Code “MA04” on 835 Electronic Remittance Advice, or
• Heath Care Claim Status Code “171” on a 277 Claim Status Response.
Identifying Recipients with Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only Coverage:
When verifying eligibility using the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS), enter the generic Service
Type Code “30” on requests, if the member has Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only coverage, the
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) response will return the message “MEDICARE
COINSURANCE DEDUCTIBLE ONLY”.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2021 Volume 37 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
***Enrollment in this Medicare type is required when the pharmacy carries one or more items that would be required to be billed
to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text: If all three conditions are not met, the billing provider may call the Department for assistance.

policy.text:
E. General Billing Guidelines for Dual Eligible Enrollees
Pursuant to federal law, Medicaid is the payer of last resort, which means Medicaid will make payments only
after all other sources of reimbursement have been exhausted. Therefore, potential third-party reimbursement
sources including Medicare, must be billed prior to billing Medicaid. For additional information, providers can
refer to the following NYS Medicaid billing guidance for dual enrollees:
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS “Duals Reimbursement in MMC” memorandum, located at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/managed_care/plans/docs/2021-12-01_duals_reimbursement
_mmc.pdf.
• NYS DOH, OMH, OASAS Medicaid Managed Care Billing Guidance for Dual Eligible Enrollees policy
guidance, located at: https://omh.ny.gov/omhweb/bho/policy-guidance/billing_for_dual_eligible_enroll
ees.pdf.
For dually enrolled Medicare and NYS Medicaid members, if Medicare covers the telehealth encounter, NYS
Medicaid will reimburse the Part B coinsurance and deductible to the extent permitted by NYS law.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Medicaid Update Volume 39 Number 1
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
6

Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only Coverage Reminder
When providing services to Medicaid recipients enrolled with Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only
coverage, Medicaid will only consider reimbursement of patient responsibility amounts of Medicare
coinsurance and/or deductible amounts.
Additionally, if Medicare did not approve the claims, or services are provided that are not statutorily covered
by Medicare, then the secondary claims submitted to Medicaid will be denied for:
• Edit 01027 (Medicaid Coverage code “09”-Medicare approved Amount Missing)
• Claim Adjustment Reason Code “16”,
• Remark Code “MA04” on 835 Electronic Remittance Advice, or
• Heath Care Claim Status Code “171” on a 277 Claim Status Response.
Identifying Recipients with Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only Coverage:
When verifying eligibility using the Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS), enter the generic Service
Type Code “30” on requests, if the member has Medicare Coinsurance and Deductible Only coverage, the
Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) response will return the message “MEDICARE
COINSURANCE DEDUCTIBLE ONLY”.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.

to Medicare on behalf of a dual-eligible member.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
This could
involve prior authorization (PA) requirements, appeal processes, or changes to medications ordered
to align with the primary plan formulary products, etc. Providers should refer to the Reminder: Pharmacy
Billing for Medicaid Patients with other Coverage article published in the November 2021 issue of the Medicaid
Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_
nov21_pr.pdf, for more information regarding this topic. If all attempts for coverage have been exhausted and
coverage has not been granted from the primary insurer, NYS DOH may consider the denial under special
circumstances. The medication in question would still be subject to any editing requirements under the NYS
Medicaid FFS program. Submitting Third Party Liability (TPL) -covered claims that incorrectly bypass the TPL
Plan responsibility of payment is considered inaccurate billing and may be subject to audit recoveries.
Questions and Additional Information:
• All questions regarding this update should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update February 2019 Volume 35 Number 3
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_36.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities.
* Provider manual rules outline specific requirements for billing and reimbursement.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid covers home infusion drugs when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Providers must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Reminders: • It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. • It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.]

**Provider Manual Definition**

Home infusion drugs must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit, and providers can refer to the correct billing guidance in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines. Pharmacy dispensing of drugs requiring administration by a practitioner is available through both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0143]-[pages: [35, 36]] - [Chunk Content: Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf]

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Medicaid policy documents provide specific criteria for home infusion drug coverage, while provider manual rules outline general billing requirements.
* Both sources emphasize the importance of timely billing and active enrollment in TPL.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0040]-[pages: [12]] - [Chunk Content: Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.]

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence exists in the provider manual context chunks regarding specific billing requirements for home infusion drugs.

infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_37.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities.
* Provider manual rules outline specific requirements for billing and reimbursement.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid covers home infusion drugs when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Providers must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Reminders: • It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. • It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.]

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the NYS Medicaid Program Customer Service Center at 1-800-322-1200.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.]

**Provider Manual Definition**

Home infusion drugs must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0071]-[pages: [19]] - [Chunk Content: Home Infusion The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.]

Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0143]-[pages: [35, 36]] - [Chunk Content: Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf]

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both Medicaid policy documents and provider manual rules emphasize the importance of timely billing and active enrollment in TPL.
* However, Medicaid policy documents provide more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage criteria.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0040]-[pages: [12]] - [Chunk Content: Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.]

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence exists in the provider manual context chunks regarding specific billing requirements for home infusion drugs.

policy.text:
Reminders:
• It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly
seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients.
• It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial
plan, Medicare, or Medicaid. Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny
.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.
• All pharmacies that are not required to bill the facility (pharmacy benefit not included in the rate) must
submit their transactions through the online Prospective Drug Utilization Review (Pro-DUR) program
(https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/ProDUR-ECCA_Provider_Manual/index.aspx)
using the National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) transaction format. NCPDP
format specifications can be found in the ProDUR ECCA Standards Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/HIPAA/5010/transactions/NCPDP_D.0_Companion_Guide.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
This could
involve prior authorization (PA) requirements, appeal processes, or changes to medications ordered
to align with the primary plan formulary products, etc. Providers should refer to the Reminder: Pharmacy
Billing for Medicaid Patients with other Coverage article published in the November 2021 issue of the Medicaid
Update, located at: https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/update/2021/docs/mu_no13_
nov21_pr.pdf, for more information regarding this topic. If all attempts for coverage have been exhausted and
coverage has not been granted from the primary insurer, NYS DOH may consider the denial under special
circumstances. The medication in question would still be subject to any editing requirements under the NYS
Medicaid FFS program. Submitting Third Party Liability (TPL) -covered claims that incorrectly bypass the TPL
Plan responsibility of payment is considered inaccurate billing and may be subject to audit recoveries.
Questions and Additional Information:
• All questions regarding this update should be directed to the Medicaid Pharmacy Policy Unit by
telephone at (518) 486-3209 or by email at NYRx@health.ny.gov.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2022 Volume 38 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Per New York State Medicaid policy, drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous
methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those
recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Additional information regarding coverage criteria
and billing guidelines for LUXTURNA™ are outlined in the March 2018 issue of the Medicaid Update.
Questions
• Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the Office of Health
Insurance Programs (OHIP) Division of Program Development and Management at (518) 473-2160.
• Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) general coverage questions may be directed to OHIP Division of Health
Plan Contracting and Oversight at covques@health.ny.gov or (518) 473-1134.
• MMC reimbursement and/or billing requirement questions should be directed to the enrollee's MMC plan.
• FFS claim questions should be directed to the eMedNY call center at (800) 343–9000.

provider_manual.text:
Home Infusion
The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services
associated with home infusion treatments. Home infusion drugs and supplies must be billed as a pharmacy or medical
benefit.
The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at:
https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.
The Centers for Medicare and Medicaid Services (CMS) requires coverage of home infusion drugs under Medicare Part
D that are not currently covered under Parts A and B of Medicare. Information on Medicare coverage of home
infusion can be found here: https://www.cms.gov/Medicare/Medicare-Fee-for-Service-Payment/Home-Infusion-
Therapy/Overview.html.

https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_40.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on home infusion drug coverage and billing responsibilities.
* Provider manual rules outline specific requirements for billing and reimbursement.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid covers home infusion drugs when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia. Providers must actively seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients, and pharmacies are responsible for timely billing to the appropriate party.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Reminders: • It is the responsibility of the providers, both the facility and the pharmacy, to actively and regularly seek TPL enrollment and Medicaid eligibility for their patients. • It is the pharmacy’s responsibility to bill timely and to the appropriate party, i.e., the facility, commercial plan, Medicare, or Medicaid.]

Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) coverage and policy questions may be directed to the NYS Medicaid Program Customer Service Center at 1-800-322-1200.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Information regarding timely billing can be found at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/Guide_to_Timely_Billing.pdf.]

**Provider Manual Definition**

Home infusion drugs must be billed as a pharmacy or medical benefit. The list of Medicaid reimbursable drugs available as a pharmacy benefit may be accessed at: https://www.emedny.org/info/formfile.aspx.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0071]-[pages: [19]] - [Chunk Content: Home Infusion The New York State Medicaid Program does not provide a bundled payment to cover drugs, supplies and services associated with home infusion treatments.]

Drugs administered by subcutaneous, intramuscular, or intravenous methods are covered when provided for Federal Drug Administration (FDA)-approved indications and those recognized off-label indications listed in the drug compendia.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0143]-[pages: [35, 36]] - [Chunk Content: Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf]

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Medicaid policy documents provide more specific guidance on home infusion drug coverage criteria, such as the requirement for FDA-approved indications.
* Provider manual rules outline specific requirements for billing, including the need to bill as a pharmacy or medical benefit.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12] - [Chunk ID: mu_no12_jul20.pdf::0030]-[pages: [8]] - [Chunk Content: Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit.]

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence exists in the provider manual context chunks regarding specific billing requirements for home infusion drugs.

policy.text:
Any claim issues must be resolved with the third party, including prior
authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Delivery
Delivery of prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical supplies, and durable medical equipment
(DME) is an optional service that can be provided to Medicaid member’s home or current residence including facilities
and shelters. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their
designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical supplies, or DME
items.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Members must meet certain conditions to
receive these benefits.

Medicare Part B
Medicare Part B covers doctors’ services, outpatient care and some other medical services that Part A does not cover.
Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

Information regarding Medicare Part B outpatient covered drugs can be found here:
https://www.medicare.gov/coverage/prescription-drugs-outpatient.
Medicare Part B covers certain drugs such as:
• Drugs used with an item of durable medical equipment.
• Some antigens,
• Injectable osteoporosis drugs,
• Erythropoiesis-stimulating agents,
• Blood clotting factors,
• Injectable and infused drugs,
• Oral End-Stage Renal Disease (ESRD) drugs,
• Parenteral and enteral nutrition (intravenous and tube feeding)
• Intravenous Immune Globulin (IVIG) provided in the home,
• Vaccinations,
• Immunosuppressive drugs following a Medicare paid transplant,
• Oral cancer drugs,
• Oral anti-nausea drugs,
• Self-administered drugs in hospital outpatient setting.
For Medicaid/Medicare crossover claims, even for a procedure that would have required Medicaid prior approval, prior
approval is not required if Medicare approved and paid for a service and/or procedure.

authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary insurer does
not cover a medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the third party will
result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
When a member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs (Part B coverage information can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers), or
• Medicare Part D or a Medicare Advantage Prescription Drug (MAPD) contracting plan (Part C)
for other prescription drugs (Part D coverage information can be found at: https://www.medicare
gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
When a member has Medicare Parts A or B or both and is not enrolled in Medicare Part D, providers will bill
Part D covered claims to the Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET) program per: https://www.
cms.gov/Medicare/Eligibility-and-Enrollment/LowIncSubMedicarePresCov/MedicareLimitedIncomeNET.html
to initiate Medicare Part D enrollment and also facilitate payment of claim.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update July 2020 Volume 36 Number 12

policy.text:
New York State Medicaid is always the payor of last resort; every effort must be taken to
obtain correct billing information.
If TPL is applicable, providers must the third-party insurer first, including commercial plans or Medicare,
and bill Medicaid as secondary, if appropriate. Any claim issues must be resolved with the third-party insurer,
including prior authorization (PA) requirements, prior to submitting the claim to Medicaid. Note: If the primary
insurer does not cover a specific medication, Medicaid will not cover the claim. Failure to submit the claim to the
third-party insurer will result in denial of the claim by Medicaid.
If the member has Medicare, providers will bill:
• Medicare Part B for Part B covered drugs. Information on Part B covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/what-medicare-covers/what-part-b-covers, or
• Medicare Part D for other prescription drugs. Information on Part D covered drugs can be found at:
https://www.medicare.gov/drug-coverage-part-d.
If the member has Medicare Parts A or B (or both) and does not have Medicare Part D, providers will bill:
• Part D covered claims to Medicare Limited Income Newly Eligible Transition (LINET).

policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Two: Pharmacy Carve-Out

provider_manual.text:
Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products in the
Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Parenteral
Examples of non-acceptable parenteral compound claim submissions are those:
• inconsistent with sterile compound criteria as required by State or federal law or regulation (8NYCRR Part 29.2
(13)), or

• made to simply dilute, reconstitute, or otherwise prepare a medication for infusion per it´s labeling, or
• products made for nutrition or hydration. Providers can refer to the correct billing guidance for these products
in the Medical Supplies Procedure Codes & Coverage Guidelines found here:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/DME/PDFS/MedicalSupply_Procedure_Codes.pdf
Pharmacy Billing
Per Medicaid policy, when billing a compound via National Council for Prescription Drug Programs (NCPDP) D.0
transaction, providers must submit a minimum of two ingredients, or National Drug Codes (NDCs), in the Compound
Segment, field 489-TE (Compound Product ID). Providers can submit up to 25 NDCs using this field.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Delivery
Delivery of prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical supplies, and durable medical equipment
(DME) is an optional service that can be provided to Medicaid member’s home or current residence including facilities
and shelters. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their
designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical supplies, or DME
items.

test_id_46.query_content: Compare Medicaid versus provider manual guidance for drugs that are covered by Medicaid but limited or excluded under Medicare pharmacy benefits.
test_id_46.concept: Medicaid-covered drugs limited or excluded under Medicaid pharmacy benefits

policy.text:
April 2022
Volume 38 | Number 4
Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in
Pharmacy and Medical Benefits
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs
cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs. There are, however, certain
drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as
outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located on the Social Security Administration “Payment
for Covered Outpatient Drugs” website, at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm and in the
eMedNY New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines document,
located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Over-the-Counter Drugs:
• NYRx covers certain over-the-counter (OTC) drugs for dual eligible NYS Medicaid members if
coverage is not available under Medicare.
• Only NYRx reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare law are covered for dual eligible NYS Medicaid
members. Therefore, NYRx does not cover OTC insulin and some OTC products which have legend
drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D and MAPD drug plans.
• All OTC products covered by NYRx with a prescription or fiscal order can be found using the search
tool, located on the Medicaid Member Dashboard “Search for OTC and Prescription Drugs” web page,
at: https://member.emedny.org/pharmacy/search-drugs. Please note: Search results may include
OTC products in the excluded categories listed above that are not covered for dual eligible NYS
Medicaid members.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Scope of Benefits
The FFS Pharmacy Carve-Out will not change the scope (e.g. copayments, covered drugs, etc.) of the
existing Medicaid Pharmacy benefit, which includes:
• Covered outpatient prescription and over-the-counter (OTC) drugs that are listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs;
• Pharmacist administered vaccines and supplies listed in the Pharmacy Procedures and Supply Codes
document, such as enteral and parenteral nutrition, family planning, and medical/surgical supplies.
The complete list of items subject to the Pharmacy Benefit Carve-Out can be reviewed in the Carve-Out
Scope web page. Please Note: Physician administered (J-Code) drugs that are not listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and supplies
(DMEPOS) as listed in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics and
Supplies Manual are not subject to the carve-out.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part One: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_46.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on drugs covered by Medicaid but limited or excluded under Medicare pharmacy benefits.
* Provider manual rules outline general requirements for dispensing and billing.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid covers certain prescription drugs not covered by Medicare Part D, including select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs. Pharmacies must bill Medicaid for these drugs unless the patient is also eligible for Medicare Part D.

policy.text:
April 2022
Volume 38 | Number 4
Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in
Pharmacy and Medical Benefits
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs
cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs. There are, however, certain
drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as
outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located on the Social Security Administration “Payment
for Covered Outpatient Drugs” website, at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm and in the
eMedNY New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines document,
located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Over-the-Counter Drugs:
• NYRx covers certain over-the-counter (OTC) drugs for dual eligible NYS Medicaid members if
coverage is not available under Medicare.
• Only NYRx reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare law are covered for dual eligible NYS Medicaid
members. Therefore, NYRx does not cover OTC insulin and some OTC products which have legend
drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D and MAPD drug plans.
• All OTC products covered by NYRx with a prescription or fiscal order can be found using the search
tool, located on the Medicaid Member Dashboard “Search for OTC and Prescription Drugs” web page,
at: https://member.emedny.org/pharmacy/search-drugs. Please note: Search results may include
OTC products in the excluded categories listed above that are not covered for dual eligible NYS
Medicaid members.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Scope of Benefits
The FFS Pharmacy Carve-Out will not change the scope (e.g. copayments, covered drugs, etc.) of the
existing Medicaid Pharmacy benefit, which includes:
• Covered outpatient prescription and over-the-counter (OTC) drugs that are listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs;
• Pharmacist administered vaccines and supplies listed in the Pharmacy Procedures and Supply Codes
document, such as enteral and parenteral nutrition, family planning, and medical/surgical supplies.
The complete list of items subject to the Pharmacy Benefit Carve-Out can be reviewed in the Carve-Out
Scope web page. Please Note: Physician administered (J-Code) drugs that are not listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and supplies
(DMEPOS) as listed in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics and
Supplies Manual are not subject to the carve-out.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part One: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

April 2022
Volume 38 | Number 4
Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in
Pharmacy and Medical Benefits
The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs
cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs. There are, however, certain
drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as
outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2), located on the Social Security Administration “Payment
for Covered Outpatient Drugs” website, at: https://www.ssa.gov/OP_Home/ssact/title19/1927.htm and in the
eMedNY New York State Medicaid Fee-for-Service Program Pharmacy Manual Policy Guidelines document,
located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/Pharmacy/PDFS/Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf.
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Over-the-Counter Drugs:
• NYRx covers certain over-the-counter (OTC) drugs for dual eligible NYS Medicaid members if
coverage is not available under Medicare.
• Only NYRx reimbursable drugs excluded by Medicare law are covered for dual eligible NYS Medicaid
members. Therefore, NYRx does not cover OTC insulin and some OTC products which have legend
drug substitutes that are covered by Medicare Part D and MAPD drug plans.
• All OTC products covered by NYRx with a prescription or fiscal order can be found using the search
tool, located on the Medicaid Member Dashboard “Search for OTC and Prescription Drugs” web page,
at: https://member.emedny.org/pharmacy/search-drugs. Please note: Search results may include
OTC products in the excluded categories listed above that are not covered for dual eligible NYS
Medicaid members.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part Three: NYRx Pharmacy Benefit Transition Volume 39 Number 4
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Scope of Benefits
The FFS Pharmacy Carve-Out will not change the scope (e.g. copayments, covered drugs, etc.) of the
existing Medicaid Pharmacy benefit, which includes:
• Covered outpatient prescription and over-the-counter (OTC) drugs that are listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs;
• Pharmacist administered vaccines and supplies listed in the Pharmacy Procedures and Supply Codes
document, such as enteral and parenteral nutrition, family planning, and medical/surgical supplies.
The complete list of items subject to the Pharmacy Benefit Carve-Out can be reviewed in the Carve-Out
Scope web page. Please Note: Physician administered (J-Code) drugs that are not listed on the Medicaid
Pharmacy List of Reimbursable Drugs and durable medical equipment, prosthetics, orthotics, and supplies
(DMEPOS) as listed in sections 4.4, 4.5, 4.6, and 4.7 of the Durable Medical Equipment, Prosthetics and
Supplies Manual are not subject to the carve-out.
policy.title: Medicaid Update Special Edition Part One: Pharmacy Carve-Out
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
The total Medicare/Medicaid payment to the pharmacy provider will not exceed the amount that the pharmacy
provider would have received for a Medicaid-only patient. If the Medicare payment is greater than the Medicaid fee,
no additional payment will be made.
Note: The Medicare and Medicaid payment (if any) must be accepted as payment in full. Per state regulation, a
pharmacy provider of a Medicare Part B benefit cannot seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible, or coinsurance
amounts from Medicare/Medicaid Dually Eligible Individuals.
Medicare Part D
Medicare members who also have Medicaid, also known as dual-eligible, must be enrolled in a Medicare Part D
prescription drug plan to maintain their Medicaid coverage. Medicaid does not cover any class of drugs covered under
Medicare Part D for full-benefit dual eligible members. Members and their prescribers must work together to find an
appropriate drug that is covered by the Part D plan or, if necessary, use the Part D plan’s exception and appeals
process to obtain coverage for necessary prescriptions not listed on the plan’s formulary.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
It is not appropriate to bill
Medicaid for any prescription drug for a Medicare Part D member unless as stated below.
Under the Medicare Part D prescription drug benefit most drug costs are paid for by Medicare. Medicaid only pays for
drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being
covered under the Part D plans, such as:
• Select prescription vitamins. and
• Certain non-prescription drugs.
All claims for dual-eligible members submitted to Medicaid are subject to Medicaid rules, including prior authorization.

For more information regarding Medicare Part D benefit, refer to the DOH website at:
https://www.health.ny.gov/health_care/medicaid/program/medicaid_transition/index.htm.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner
NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a
health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit. Drugs are
evaluated to determine if they are eligible for coverage under the pharmacy benefit using the following criteria:
• The drug must be able to be billed in the NCPDP format.
• The drug must be able to be delivered directly to the place of administration. (See Delivery section)
• The drug must not be included in the rate for a facility.
• The drug must be available from a dispensing pharmacy that is enrolled in Medicaid.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and state law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and state law,
rule, or regulation.
• In certain circumstances, the DUR Board may need to review the drug before clinical criteria may be
applied.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_50.answers:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on drugs covered by Medicaid but limited or excluded under Medicare pharmacy benefits.
* Provider manual rules outline general requirements for dispensing and billing.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid covers certain prescription drugs not covered by Medicare Part D, including select prescription vitamins and non-prescription drugs. Pharmacies must bill Medicaid for these drugs unless the patient is also eligible for Medicare Part D.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4] - [Chunk ID: mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0000]-[pages: [1]] - [Chunk Content: ... certain drug/drug classes that are not covered by Medicaid for certain diagnoses due to federal or state rules as outlined in both the Social Security Act §1927(d)(2)...]
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070]-[pages: [18, 19]] - [Chunk Content: ... Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans...]

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacies must accept Medicare payment as payment in full and not seek to recover any Medicare Part B deductible or coinsurance amounts. Pharmacies can bill Medicaid for prescription drugs not covered by Medicare Part D, but only if the patient is also eligible for Medicaid.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0040]-[pages: [12]] - [Chunk Content: ... Pharmacy Dispensing of Drugs That Require Administration by a Practitioner NYS Medicaid recognizes the need for certain drugs, including home infusion drugs, requiring administration by a health care provider to be available to members by way of both the medical benefit and pharmacy benefit...]

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Policy documents provide more specific guidance on which drug classes are excluded from coverage under Medicare Part D.
* Provider manual rules outline general requirements for dispensing and billing, but do not provide detailed information on specific drug classes.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Update April 2022 Volume 38 Number 4] - [Chunk ID: mu_no4_apr22_pr.pdf::0000]-[pages: [1]] - [Chunk Content: ... April 2022 Volume 38 | Number 4 Reminder: Some Drug Categories are Excluded from Coverage in Pharmacy and Medical Benefits The New York State (NYS) Medicaid fee-for-service (FFS) and Medicaid Managed Care (MMC) programs cover an extensive drug benefit providing access to medically necessary drugs.]
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] - [Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0070]-[pages: [18, 19]] - [Chunk Content: ... Medicaid only pays for drugs from the List of Medicaid Reimbursable Drugs where the drug class is specifically excluded by law from being covered under the Part D plans...]

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence exists in provider manual context chunks regarding specific requirements for dispensing and billing for drugs that are covered by Medicaid but limited or excluded under Medicare pharmacy benefits.
